hq: 2261 Market Street, STE 85434, San Francisco, CA 94114

system_description.overview: AgentMail provides an API-first email infrastructure platform designed to give AI agents their own dedicated inboxes at scale. The platform enables developers to programmatically create thousands of ephemeral inboxes, send and receive emails, and stream structured JSON events in real time. It solves limitations of traditional email providers by eliminating per-inbox costs, API rate limits, and domain reputation risks.

products.Automated Email Infrastructure Managemen: Programmatic provisioning of dedicated email inboxes at scale, with per-domain and per-inbox isolation. Automated domain warm-up, SPF/DKIM/DMARC configuration, and deliverability monitoring across multiple sending environments.
products.Real-Time Messaging and Event Processing: Structured JSON events for every inbound and outbound message to enable traceability, replay, and compliance reporting. Webhooks and API endpoints for secure, auditable message ingestion, storage, and retrieval with defined retention policies.
products.Security and Compliance Controls: Encryption of all data in transit and at rest, access control policies for customer data, and per-tenant data segregation. Configurable data retention and deletion settings with logging and audit trails.
products.Operational Monitoring and Incident Resp: Real-time monitoring of message flow, system health, and deliverability metrics. Documented incident response procedures, security alerting, and breach notification workflows.
products.Integration and Support Services: API-based integrations for customer applications and ML workflows, with structured data export capabilities. Dedicated onboarding, support, and security reviews for enterprise customers.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (carve-out method - AWS controls excluded from scope)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - 100% cloud-hosted, no physical servers or offices in scope

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Segmented VPCs with access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality (CC6.1.7).
network_architecture.api_gateway: Application gateway (firewalls configured on application gateway and production network)
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on the application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management. Firewall access restricted to authorized personnel (CC6.6.1-CC6.6.3).
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS employed via AWS to identify and protect against threats. Intrusion detection system utilized to monitor and detect unauthorized access or anomalies within the network (CC2.1.1, CC6.6.1).
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access done through a bastion host with a virtual private network (VPN).
network_architecture.tls: Transport Layer Security (TLS) using certificates from an established third-party certificate authority.
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; all systems restricted to internet through content filtering.
network_architecture.topology: Internet -> TLS encryption -> Application gateway with firewalls -> VPC with segmented subnets -> us-west-1 and us-east-1 regions each containing compute instances, Lambda functions, and database services -> Shared data layer. Remote admin access via VPN through bastion host.

application_architecture.pattern: Hybrid (serverless Lambda + virtual servers visible in network diagram)
compute: Virtual servers (visible in network diagram, specific service not named)
application_architecture.api_style: REST (API-first platform with webhooks and JSON event streaming)
application_architecture.environments: Segregated development/test and production environments. Changes tested in separate environment before production deployment (CC8.1.4).

databases: AWS managed database (visible in network diagram in both regions, specific engine not named)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: AWS IAM / Active Directory (referenced in CC6.1.2 context of AD, server, and database accounts)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1). MFA enforced for all remote access (CC6.1.1 test results). MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3). MFA required for employee email access.
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through AWS password policy with periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements. Accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts. Guest and anonymous logins not allowed. Unattended desktops locked within a time of inactivity. Password or biometrics required to unlock.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service (AWS KMS implied - CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: AWS KMS (cloud provider key management service; access to encryption keys restricted to authorized personnel - CC6.1.9)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool (specific vendor not named; code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed to author with MFA access - CC8.1.3)
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Not explicitly named; internal tool tracks changes from initiation through deployment and validation
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move change to production requires additional approval (CC8.1.2).
ci_cd_devops.iac: Infrastructure as code with configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback capability (CC7.1.1)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management with monthly reviews by CTO. Critical/security-related OS patches applied as they become available. Workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance (CC7.5.1).

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Monitoring tool (not named; generates alerts when predefined thresholds are met - CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1).
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events. Incident management process invoked for confirmed events; logs and alerts tracked until resolved within change-management/ticketing application.

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure provider (data center, compute, networking, storage, key management, serverless)
TLS certificate issuance for web-based systems

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers to prevent, detect, and act upon unauthorized or malicious software. Continuous automatic scanning. Virus definitions updated weekly for production servers. Anti-virus also installed on Mac-based workstations and laptops (CC6.8.1).
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated scans on infrastructure and applications with frequency adjusted as required. Remediation plan developed for critical and high vulnerabilities (CC7.1.2).
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party. Results reviewed by IT management. High/critical findings remediated and added to risk register with risk treatment plan (CC4.1.1).

untestable: CC2.2.3 - Communication of significant changes to people/roles could not be tested (no significant changes during engagement period)
CC3.2.1 - Cybersecurity insurance effectiveness could not be tested (no cyber incidents during engagement period)
CC6.5.2 - Customer data deletion upon termination could not be tested (no customer terminations during engagement period)
CC7.3.1 - Incident response policies effectiveness could not be tested (no security incidents during engagement period)
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access to facilities excluded; AWS responsible for data center physical security
A1.2 - Environmental protections and recovery infrastructure excluded; no office, controls managed by AWS/building management
Processing Integrity - Determined not relevant to the system by AgentMail
Privacy - Determined not relevant to the system by AgentMail
notable_controls: Risk and Governance Executive Committee (RGEC) meets semiannually to review key internal control measures; IT Leadership Committee meets weekly (CC2.1.2, CC3.1.1)
Chief Information Security Officer role assigned for incident response oversight (CC7.4)
Per-tenant data segregation enforced at infrastructure level (CC6.1.3)
Formal vendor management program with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports (CC9.2.1)
Data classification policy categorizing data by sensitivity and criticality (CC2.1.3)
Infrastructure as code with configuration management tool and rollback capability (CC7.1.1)
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and backup failure alerting (CC7.4.2)

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-region (us-west-1, us-east-1), multi-AZ deployment on AWS with IaC and failover capabilities. Loses points for no CDN, no WAF, single cloud provider with carve-out (no direct control visibility over AWS infrastructure), and limited specifics on compute/storage services beyond what the network diagram shows.

application_architecture.rationale: Hybrid pattern with Lambda serverless and virtual servers. API-first design with webhooks and JSON streaming is modern. Branch protection and code review confirmed. However, zero programming languages, frameworks, or specific compute services are named anywhere in the report, making it impossible to assess code quality or maintainability.

data_layer.rationale: Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed. Data classification and retention policies exist. Per-tenant segregation is strong for a multi-tenant email platform. Loses points because no specific database engines, caching layers, file storage services, or data warehouse are named.

security_posture.rationale: Strong controls: IDS/IPS, firewalls on application gateway, annual external pen testing, monthly vulnerability scanning, endpoint protection with anti-malware, MFA for admin/remote/source code access, encryption at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS), cybersecurity insurance, network segmentation, VPN via bastion host. Loses points for no named WAF, no named SIEM, and generic tool references throughout.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC with configuration management and rollback confirmed. Branch protection with merge approval required. Separate environments. Emergency change process documented. Formal change management lifecycle with testing before production. Loses points because no specific CI/CD tool, source control platform, or container orchestration is named.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing across different loss scenarios. Multi-AZ and multi-region failover (spinning up servers across availability zones). Daily backups with failure alerting. Loses points for no disclosed RTO/RPO targets and reliance on AWS subservice for all physical/environmental controls.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Near-total dependency on AWS for all infrastructure, compute, storage, key management, IDS/IPS, and data center services. No additional cloud providers, CDN vendors, or named third-party SaaS tools disclosed. Only AWS and a certificate authority are identifiable technology vendors. This represents significant concentration risk.

overall.rationale: AgentMail demonstrates a solid security-conscious posture for an early-stage company with a clean SOC 2 Type 2 report (zero exceptions on testable controls). Multi-region AWS infrastructure with IaC, comprehensive access controls, and formal BCDR planning are strengths. However, the report is notably light on technology specifics across the board. The heavy AWS concentration and minimal vendor diversity are material risks. The short 3-month audit period and four untestable controls limit the assurance value of the report.

red_flags: Extremely short audit observation period (3 months: Aug 7 - Nov 7, 2025) - the minimum typical for a Type 2 report, suggesting controls may have been recently established
Four controls untestable due to no events during engagement period (CC2.2.3, CC3.2.1, CC6.5.2, CC7.3.1) - their operating effectiveness is unverified
Near-total dependency on a single cloud provider (AWS) with carve-out method meaning AWS controls are explicitly excluded from assurance scope
No specific technology stack disclosed anywhere in the report - zero programming languages, database engines, CI/CD tools, monitoring platforms, or SIEM named
yellow_flags: No WAF (Web Application Firewall) mentioned despite being an API-first platform handling email traffic at scale
No named SIEM or log aggregation platform - monitoring tools referenced only generically
No RTO or RPO disclosed despite having a BCDR policy and annual testing program
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust service categories excluded as 'not relevant' despite handling email content for AI agents
Cybersecurity insurance control (CC3.2.1) existence confirmed but effectiveness untested
No CDN mentioned for an API-first platform presumably serving global developer customers
Section 5 (Other Information) is minimal - only a brief DR description with no additional technical detail
Report contains significant control description repetition across criteria suggesting heavy use of compliance automation templates
green_flags: Clean unqualified opinion with zero exceptions on all testable controls
Multi-region (us-west-1 and us-east-1) multi-AZ architecture with failover capabilities
Per-tenant data segregation enforced at infrastructure level - critical for a multi-tenant email platform
Comprehensive access control: MFA for admin/remote/source code access, RBAC, quarterly access reviews, 1-business-day termination revocation SLA
Infrastructure as code with configuration management tool and rollback capability
Formal BCDR policy with annual testing across different loss scenarios
Branch protection with required merge approval and source code changes logged/timestamped/attributed
Annual external penetration testing with remediation tracking in risk register
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with adjustable frequency
Formal vendor management program with SOC report reviews and quarterly assessments for vendors without SOC reports
Data classification policy established and implemented by sensitivity/criticality
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and backup failure alerting
Risk and Governance Executive Committee (RGEC) with IT Leadership Committee meeting weekly
key_observations: The report is heavily template-driven with significant control description repetition across criteria (e.g., CC1.4.1 text appears verbatim under CC1.2, CC2.2, CC2.3, CC3.4), suggesting a compliance-automation platform was used to generate it

Despite being an email infrastructure company, the report contains zero mention of email-specific security technologies (DKIM key management, SPF/DMARC infrastructure, IP reputation management) in the controls section - these appear only in product marketing descriptions

The network diagram shows AWS Lambda icons in both regions alongside compute and database icons, suggesting a hybrid serverless architecture, but controls never reference serverless-specific considerations
The org chart shows only CEO, CTO, and COO - the CISO role referenced in CC7.4 context may be held by one of these executives rather than a dedicated hire

The company appears very early-stage: no physical office, minimal organizational structure, first SOC 2 with minimum observation period, no significant changes during audit period, and no customer terminations - suggesting limited operational history

The carve-out of AWS means the most critical infrastructure controls (physical security, network backbone, hypervisor security) are explicitly outside the assurance boundary - evaluators must separately examine AWS's own SOC 2 report
